figures.gloss: The bright sky and high god of the Balts, whose very name is the unbroken cognate of *Dyeus — the same word as Zeus, Jupiter, Dyaus, Tiwaz, worn smooth across five thousand years.
figures.facet: The shard that kept the word: where Tyr decayed and Zeus was renamed, the Baltic peasant still said *Dievas* into the twentieth century — the longest-surviving utterance of the Sky-Father's true name.
figures.provenance: Reconstructed from Lithuanian and Latvian folksong (dainas) collected 18th–20th c.; the late Christianisation of the Balts preserved an unusually archaic layer.

figures.gloss: The thunder-god who rides the sky in a fiery chariot and splits the oak, his name from *Perkwunos — the near-twin of Slavic Perun and structural brother to Indra and Thor.
figures.facet: Keeper of the oldest storm-myth still living in folklore: Perkūnas hunts Velnias the serpent across the sky and through every tree and stone the devil hides behind — the IE thunder-vs-serpent combat told as a peasant chase.

figures.gloss: The Sun as a goddess and mother, who drives her copper-wheeled chariot across the sky, weeps amber tears into the sea, and tends the cosmic apple-tree at the world's edge.
figures.facet: The divergence that overturns a habit: where most pantheons make the sun a king or charioteer-god, the Balts kept the Sun female and maternal — a feminine solar eye, complement not consort to the moon.

figures.gloss: The Moon, husband of the Sun, who weds Saulė at the world's beginning and then, faithless, courts the dawn-star Aušrinė — for which Perkūnas cleaves him, and his wound is the moon's waning.
figures.facet: A cosmic divorce written into the calendar: the Sun-and-Moon marriage, their quarrel, and the Moon's punishment explain the phases as the scar of an infidelity — myth as the reckoning of time itself.

figures.gloss: The Earth-Mother, the blossoming ground from whom all life rises and to whom it returns; her name shares the root *dhg'hem- with Greek chthon and Latin humus — the Earth herself.
figures.facet: Kissed at dawn and at death: the Lithuanian farmer greeted Žemyna by kissing the soil and poured the first of every drink to her — a great-mother worshipped not in temple but in the daily act of touching the ground.

figures.gloss: Goddess of fate, fortune and birth, who lays down each life's measure at the threshold; her decree is *lemtis*, the allotted portion no god overturns.
figures.facet: Fate as a midwife, not a spinner: Laima stands at the moment of birth to speak the lot, and even Dievas defers to what she has set — the impersonal allotment that order itself obeys.

figures.gloss: A scholarly reconstruction, assembled from post-Christian folklore fused with medieval chronicle glosses, casts Velnias as lord of the dead, of cattle, water and the dark below — the serpentine chthonic power whom Perkūnas eternally hunts; the figure was later flattened by Christianity into the folk-devil, and no single pre-Christian source states this full portfolio at once.
figures.facet: The slandered sovereign: Velnias was not evil but the underworld's rightful master of wealth and the dead, demoted to 'the Devil' only when the new faith needed an enemy for its God — a shard cracked by conversion.
figures.provenance: The pre-Christian Velnias/Vels must be read beneath the later Christianised folk-devil; the chthonic, cattle-keeping layer is the genuinely archaic one (cf. Latvian Veļi, the dead).

figures.gloss: The 'Sons of God', the Latvian divine twins who court Saule's daughter, rescue her from the sea and steady her sinking boat — the Baltic face of the Indo-European Dawn-Twins.
figures.facet: A living witness to a reconstruction: the Dieva dēli courting the Sun-Maiden mirror the Vedic Aśvins and Greek Dioskouroi so closely that they confirm the *Diwos sunu — Sons of Dyeus — as a real inherited myth, not a guess.

figures.gloss: The Morning Star, the dawn-goddess who kindles Saulė's fire and lights her way; her name from *h2ews-, the same root as Greek Eos, Latin Aurora, Vedic Uṣas.
figures.facet: The dawn the Balts kept by name: where Eos and Aurora became literary figures, Aušrinė stayed a working morning-star whose mythic root is audible in the word itself.

figures.gloss: The Lithuanian goddess of the hearth fire, treated as a living presence within the home: fed daily with bread and salt, blessed each evening, and ritually 'banked' under ash rather than left to die out overnight.
figures.facet: Devotion enacted as housework: Gabija asks no temple, only the nightly discipline of tending and never insulting the flame — sanctity folded into an ordinary domestic chore.
figures.provenance: Attested across Lithuanian folksong (dainos) and 19th–20th c. ethnographic collection (Balys, Vėlius); the crusader-era chronicles' reports of a perpetual sacred fire at pagan sanctuaries are the same cult's public face.

figures.gloss: The sacred grass snake kept beneath or near the hearth and fed milk, believed to bring luck, fertility, and prosperity to the household that shelters it; killing one was held to bring misfortune, or to offend Saulė herself.
figures.facet: The mundane made taboo: an ordinary, harmless grass snake elevated into an untouchable house-guest — sanctity assigned not to a distant cosmic serpent but to the animal literally underfoot.
figures.provenance: Reported by 16th–19th c. travelers and chroniclers (e.g., Jan Łasicki) and confirmed in later ethnographic collection; among the best-attested elements of everyday Baltic practice.

figures.gloss: The forest goddess named among the gods of Grand Duke Mindaugas in the Hypatian Codex (13th c.) — a huntress-power of the wooded wilderness, later folklorized toward a she-wolf figure in regional folklore.
figures.facet: One of vanishingly few Baltic deities with a name preserved in a contemporary chronicle rather than reconstructed wholesale from later folklore — a chink of hard textual light in an otherwise oral record.
figures.provenance: Hypatian Codex (13th c., on Mindaugas's gods); discussed in Norbertas Vėlius's sourcebook of Baltic religion and mythology.

figures.gloss: A flying household spirit, a fiery-tailed streak across the night sky, that brings its keeper stolen grain and money; folklore holds it is bought from the devil at the price of the owner's soul, must be fed an omelette, and will burn down the house that fails to satisfy it.
figures.facet: Prosperity with a ledger attached: the aitvaras converts the neighbor's loss directly into the keeper's gain, making household luck an explicitly zero-sum and morally compromised transaction rather than a blessing.
figures.provenance: Extensively recorded in Lithuanian folktale collections (Basanavičius; later Balys); a signature figure of the tale-corpus, with regional name-variants.

figures.gloss: The vėlės are the shades of the dead, believed to linger near their living kin and revisit the home; Vėlinės, the great feast of the dead observed in early November, is when food and drink are set out and graves are visited and tended — a custom that continued through Christianization without interruption.
figures.facet: The one Baltic rite Christianity never had to reintroduce because it never actually stopped: All Souls' simply gave the vėlės feast a new calendar peg to hang on.
figures.provenance: Attested continuously from early-modern travelers' accounts to present-day observance (Vėlinės remains a living Lithuanian custom); the root is shared with Latvian veļi, 'the dead' — cf. this roster's Velnias.

figures.gloss: The mythic first man to be cremated, per a tale interpolated into the Hypatian Chronicle under the year 1261/1262: trial-buried first in the earth and then among worms, Sovijus found no rest until his sons burned his body, after which he passed through the gates of the underworld and returned to demand his descendants be sent to the afterlife by fire ever after.
figures.facet: A myth with a birth-certificate: nearly every other Baltic story survives only in folklore recorded centuries later, but Sovijus's tale is fixed to an actual chronicle date — the closest thing this tradition has to a founding scripture.
figures.provenance: The 'Sovijus' interpolation in the Hypatian Codex (a medieval Rus' chronicle compilation, entry conventionally dated 1261/1262); discussed by Algirdas Greimas and Norbertas Vėlius as the one dated indigenous Baltic myth-text.

figures.gloss: Wild fairy-women of Lithuanian folklore who spin, weave, and launder by night at streams and crossroads, reward diligent humans (especially women and children) and punish the lazy, and are said to swap or smother human infants, leaving a laumės vaikas ('laumė's child') in their place.
figures.facet: Domestic virtue policed from outside the household: the laumės enforce exactly the values — diligence, tidiness, care of children — a Baltic housewife was raised to embody, as if the culture's own work-ethic had grown teeth and left the house at night.
figures.provenance: Extensively recorded in Lithuanian folktale collection (Basanavičius, Balys); regional variants debate the overlap between deivė and laumė as spirit-classes.

figures.gloss: The alleged supreme pagan priest of the Prussians and Balts, said by the 14th-century crusader chronicler Peter of Dusburg to have presided at the sanctuary of Romuva and been 'revered like a pope' by kings and commoners alike; no independent Baltic source confirms an office of this kind.
figures.facet: A textbook case of the corpus's own evidentiary law made concrete: the Krivė is bright, dramatic, and almost certainly over-claimed — a crusader's need for a pagan 'pope' to conquer, more than a documented Baltic institution.
figures.provenance: Peter of Dusburg, 'Chronicon terrae Prussiae' (1326); treated with sustained scholarly skepticism, some scholars regarding the office as an invention or exaggeration of a lesser role.

edges.note: Lithuanian Dievas / Latvian Dievs continues *Dyeus directly — among the least-altered reflexes of the reconstructed Sky-Father's name.
Same Indo-European etymon *Dyeus; Dievas and Zeus are the same inherited word in different daughter tongues.
Dievas and Iuppiter (Dieus-pater) share the *Dyeus root by regular sound-law within Indo-European.
Dievas and Vedic Dyaus continue the same *Dyeus — the cognate star at its widest span.
Both from *Dyeus/*Tiwaz; Dievas kept the supreme sky-god role that Tyr surrendered to Odin and Thor.
Perkūnas and Perun both reflect *Perkwunos within the Balto-Slavic node — the closest cognate pair in the whole storm-cluster, oak and thunder shared.
The thunder-god who slays the serpent: Perkūnas hunting Velnias rhymes structurally with Indra and Vṛtra, though the names are not cognate.
Oak-and-thunder god wielding the bolt against a chthonic serpent — the same IE storm-template, distinct lineage from Perkūnas's *Perkwunos.
Wheel-and-thunder gods of the western and eastern IE world; both carry the *(s)tenh2- / *Perkwunos thunder-complex, no proven common name.
The defining Baltic combat: Perkūnas (sky) eternally pursues Velnias (the serpent below) — the IE thunder-vs-serpent myth preserved as living folklore.
Sun-wife and Moon-husband, their marriage and quarrel are the Baltic cosmogony of day, night and the lunar phases.
Two of the rare feminine solar deities — Saulė and Amaterasu — independent witnesses that the sun need not be male.
Both drive the solar chariot across the sky and both belong to IE solar imagery, but Saulė is mother where Sūrya is lord — the gender inverts.
Chariot-driving sun of the IE world; the Baltic Saulė keeps the wheel and the daily course while turning the charioteer female.
The Moon's faithless courting of the dawn-star Aušrinė is the offence for which Perkūnas wounds him — the myth that reads the lunar phases.
Within the Baltic sky-family Aušrinė the morning-star serves and kindles Saulė the Sun, attested together in the dainas.
Earth personified as mother and source; Žemyna and Gaia share the function and, with chthon, the *dhg'hem- earth-root — but the divine figures are parallels, not one name.
Grain-and-ground mothers of the field; the Lithuanian first-fruits to Žemyna echo Demeter's agrarian cult without proven descent.
The Dieva dēli court and rescue Saule's daughter and right her sinking boat — the Baltic Sons-of-God / Sun-Maiden cycle in one scene.
The 'Sons of God' are by name the sons of Dievas — *Diwos sunu, the divine twins begotten of the Sky-Father.
Aušrinė's root *h2ews- (dawn) is cognate with Greek Eos/Aurora, not with Helios; the seam belongs to the dawn-goddess line — flagged as a reach, true target is the dawn not the sun.
Goddesses presiding over birth, marriage and the measure of a life; Laima at the threshold rhymes with Frigg who knows all fates yet speaks none.
Both touch the allotment of life and death, but Laima is a benevolent midwife of fate where the Morrigan is its war-shadow — a loose, divergent parallel.
Wealth-keeping lords of the dead below; Velnias, master of cattle and the buried hoard, parallels Hades the rich one — independent underworld sovereigns.
First-and-foremost lords of the dead in IE daughter traditions; the Latvian Veļi (the dead) gather under Velnias as the departed gather under Yama.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Figures of spiritual disruption or underworldly attachment that must be managed, outwitted, or neutralized by practitioners.

Neighbouring storm-gods of the Baltic shore, Ukko and Perkūnas share thunder, axe and the rainbow-bow through long Balto-Finnic contact — a real areal rhyme across the IE/Uralic divide, never a common name.

Same celestial body, opposite gender: Hina is the WOMAN in the moon where Baltic Mėnulis is the Moon-husband to Saulė the Sun-wife — the moon sexed female in Polynesia, male in the Baltic; structure shared, valence flipped, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an earthbound, trickster-like adversary deeply associated with magic, music, and the dead, often syncretized later with the Christian Devil.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are luminous, ancient Indo-European dawn goddesses who survived long into folklore as figures of youth, beauty, and morning light.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both embody unruly, underworld or earthbound forces that mortals attempt to outwit, bribe, or weaponize for pragmatic gain.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A goddess who not only gives life but physically dictates and records the precise lifespan and destiny of newborns.

Northern solar divinities reached against the long winter, both frequently feminine — Beaivi the returning Sámi Sun and the Baltic sun-goddess Saulė; the solar joint filled by the warmth begged back over the dark, in two unrelated families of the same latitudes.

Moon-gods of the northern latitudes paired against the Sun — the Sámi Mánnu and the Baltic Mēnulis; the lunar joint as the ruling light of the dark season, reached in two separate families along the same cold sky.

The revived Veles carries the old Slavic name whose *wel- root scholars link with Baltic Velnias and the Latvian veļi (the dead) — a Balto-Slavic chthonic name-line (Ivanov–Toporov), encoded here to the Baltic node since the old Slavic shard holds no Veles.

The Rozhanitsy who receive the birth-meal and set the newborn's lot stand beside Baltic Laima at the threshold of birth — the birth-allotment office in the two neighboring families, a classic Balto-Slavic folklore parallel, not proven descent.

The revival-of seam at its cleanest: the dainos Romuva sings are the same collected corpus from which Saulė and the Baltic pantheon are known at all — the liturgy and the primary source are one body of song.

The rekindled sacred fire restores the attested cult-form of the old Baltic sanctuaries whose perpetual flame the chroniclers tied to the worship of Perkūnas — an attested rite revived across a six-century break, not an unbroken flame.
Both are cult-figures of the hearth itself — Gabija the fire, Žaltys the snake sheltered by its warmth — twin tutelary presences of the same physical hearth.
Killing a žaltys was said to offend Saulė, who weeps for it — the household snake stands under the Sun-goddess's own protection in the folklore record.
Folklore holds the aitvaras is purchased from Velnias at the price of the owner's soul — the household luck-spirit's wealth is chthonic credit, not a free gift.
Velnias's name and dominion share the *wel- root with vėlės/veļi, the shades he is said to preside over as lord of the dead — the etymology ties ruler to ruled.

Sovijus journeys through the underworld's gates to win the right of cremation — a mortal errand into the same chthonic domain Velnias is said to rule, though the chronicle does not name Velnias himself as its gatekeeper.
Sovijus's founding of cremation is the charter-myth that makes the vėlės' continued nearness to the living possible — the story explains the rite that Vėlinės perpetuates.
Both are female powers of the land, though Medeina's domain is the untamed forest against Žemyna's cultivated earth — sister domains, not one goddess.
Virgin huntress-powers of the untamed wild in two unrelated pantheons — Medeina's forest and Artemis's mountain, independent witnesses to the same religious function.
Cross-tradition: hearth fire tended nightly as a living, feminine, ash-banked presence — independent witnesses from opposite ends of Eurasia to the same domestic-cult logic.

Cross-tradition: Herodotus names Tabiti 'Hestia' for her place as hearth-fire queen of the Scythians — the same reverence for the tended household flame recurs, unrelated lineage, in the Baltic Gabija.

Cross-tradition: nocturnal fairy-women of unrelated substrate traditions who work at night (spinning/laundering) and must be dealt with reciprocally — the same folk-logic of the sacred-as-neighbor recurring independently in the Baltic and the Pyrenees.
Cross-tradition: two substrate traditions independently keep the sun explicitly female — daughter of the Earth, not a male charioteer-king — a real if unrelated convergence.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a pre-Christian sacred entity or holy site whose name was inverted into a demonic figure under Christianization — Hiisi's grove-to-goblin path and Velnias's shift toward 'the Devil' are parallel semantic casualties of conversion, not a shared line.
Aušrinė continues *H₂éwsōs directly by the *h2ews- root — the reconstructed ancestor node the existing Baltic shard's own flagged Ausrine–Helios edge was missing.
Baltic Perkūnas continues *Perkʷūnos directly and almost unaltered — among the least-changed reflexes of the reconstructed thunder-god's name.
Baltic Dieva dēli ('Sons of God') continue *Diwós sūnū by name and by myth — courting and rescuing the Sun-Maiden, the reconstructed twins' defining act.
Baltic Saulė continues *Seh₂ul directly, keeping both the root and the feminine gender of the reconstructed sun.
Baltic Žemyna continues the *dʰǵʰem- earth-root behind the reconstructed Earth-Mother, kissed at dawn and poured the first drink of every meal.

Neighboring earth-and-moisture mother goddesses honored with women's household offerings; the old sources give Mokosh no genealogy linking her to Baltic Žemyna, so the kinship is structural, not attested descent.

Romuva's libation and bread offering directly continues the žemyneliavimas custom the old folklore sources record — modern cult practice reaching back to the same Earth-Mother across the same revival break as the sacred fire.

Veles and Baltic Velnias share the reconstructed *wel- root (Ivanov & Toporov) tying them to a common Balto-Slavic chthonic figure — the same equation Rodnovery's revived Veles already carries to this Baltic node.

Neighboring Balto-Slavic earth-goddesses of moist ground and women's domestic labor, structurally paired the way Rod's Rozhanitsy already pair with Baltic Laima at birth — a folklore parallel, not proven descent.

dossier.essence: The pre-Christian religion of the Baltic-speaking peoples — Lithuanians, Latvians, and the extinct Old Prussians — of the eastern Baltic littoral, among the most conservative descendants of common Indo-European religion. Lithuania, the last pagan state in Europe, formally converted only in 1387 (Samogitia in 1413), and folk practice carried the old gods in song into the modern era; the tradition has been consciously revived as Lithuanian Romuva and Latvian Dievturiba.
dossier.cosmos: A bright sky-order — Dievas the sky-god, Perkunas the thunderer, Saule the sun-mother driving her chariot — stands over Zemyna's earth and against the chthonic Velnias, lord of the dead, cattle, and waters, whom Perkunas eternally pursues; the marriage of Sun and Moon, and the Moon's infidelity with the dawn-star Ausrine, account for the lunar phases. Fate is allotted at birth by Laima, and even the gods defer to the portion she speaks. The dead (veles) pass into a community of souls that remains in relation with the living, commemorated at autumn feasts of the dead.
dossier.practice: Cult was largely open-air and domestic: sacred groves (alkai), holy fires, springs, and stones rather than temple institutions; households honored Zemyna with the first of bread and drink poured to the ground, and the year turned on solstice and harvest festivals (Rasos/Jani at midsummer) and commemorations of the dead (Lithuanian Velines). Medieval chronicles report organized sanctuaries and priesthoods, but those accounts are late, external, and contested; the reliable picture is a peasant religion of field, hearth, and season.
dossier.canon: No native scriptures: the corpus is oral — above all the enormous Latvian daina collections (Krisjanis Barons' 'Latvju dainas', 1894-1915) and Lithuanian folksong, read alongside hostile medieval chronicles and comparative Indo-European reconstruction.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds sheer conservatism — the shard where Indo-European religion survived longest in living speech, its gods preserved as household words and folksong rather than doctrine.
sources: Algirdas Julien Greimas, 'Of Gods and Men: Studies in Lithuanian Mythology' (1992, English translation)
Endre Bojtar, 'Foreword to the Past: A Cultural History of the Baltic People' (1999)
